content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
C -jAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
June 6,
1995
A.J.
Yates
Deputy Secretary
California State Department
of Food and Agriculture
1220
N Street
Sacramento, California
95814
Re:
Your Written Request For
Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-95—166
Dear Mr.
Yates:
This letter responds to your May 12,
1995, letter requesting
review and approval of a draft recusal notice under the
disqualification procedures for public officials regulated by the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Although the Act requires
written documentation whenever a financial conflict causes a
public official to abstain from participation in a specific
governmental decision, this agency neither reviews nor approves
the content of such written recusal notices.
Moreover, the Act
does not require a “general” recusal notice when no decision is
pending.
Accordingly,
we are treating your letter as a request
f-or informal advice regarding the Act’s requirements for written
notice of recusal caused by a financial conflict.
-
The conflict-of—interest provisions of the Act require that a
public official disqualify himself from making, participating in
making,
or otherwise attempting to influence a governmental
decision in which it is reasonably foreseeable that the official’s
financial interest may be materially affected in a manner
distinguishable from the decision’s effects on the public
generally.
(Sections 87100,
87103.)
When a public official has
determined that the law requires his disqualification from a
governmental decision, the official’s disqualification must be
accompanied by written disclosure of the financial interest
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—95—166
Page
2
which prompts the disqualification.
(Regulation 18700(b) (5).)
The written disclosure,
in the case of appointed public officials,
must be submitted to the appointing authority.
(Regulation
18730(b) (10).)
Disclosure of the financial interest prompting disqualif i—
cation must be made with a level of specificity at least equal to
the disclosure of financial interests on the official’s annual
statement of economic interests.
Additionally, disclosure is
required each time the official determines not to act with respect
to a governmental decision on the basis of a financial conflict.
(Regulation 18700(b) (5).)
We trust that this advice is helpful.
Should you have
additional questions, please feel free to contact the undersigned.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
Couel
By:
Daniel E.
Muallem
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:DEM:ak
cc:
James Sutton,
Esq.
Law Offices of Nielsen,
Merksamer,
Parrinello, Mueller and Naylor

sections.question_synthetic: What are the FPPC requirements for written notice of recusal when a public official has a financial conflict of interest that requires disqualification from a governmental decision?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Public officials must provide written disclosure of the financial interest prompting disqualification with specificity equal to annual statement disclosures, submitted to the appointing authority for appointed officials, and this disclosure is required each time the official determines not to act due to a financial conflict. The FPPC does not review or approve recusal notices, and no general recusal notice is required when no decision is pending.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter in response to written request dated May 12, 1995. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections marked with headers, but question and answer are clearly present in body text. Includes regulatory citations and guidance on disclosure requirements.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the FPPC requirements for written notice of recusal when a public official has a financial conflict of interest that requires disqualification from a governmental decision?

CONCLUSION: Public officials must provide written disclosure of the financial interest prompting disqualification with specificity equal to annual statement disclosures, submitted to the appointing authority for appointed officials, and this disclosure is required each time the official determines not to act due to a financial conflict. The FPPC does not review or approve recusal notices, and no general recusal notice is required when no decision is pending.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a request for review of a draft recusal notice by clarifying that while the Political Reform Act requires written documentation of financial conflicts causing disqualification, the FPPC does not review or approve such notices. The agency provides guidance on the specificity and timing requirements for disclosing financial interests that prompt recusal.
